questions.question: What is the work done by a force of $6.0\text{ N}$ acting horizontally on a body of mass $4.0\text{ kg}$ if the distance moved in the direction of the force is $3.0\text{ m}$?
questions.explanation: Directly from the source: $W = F \times s = 6.0\text{ N} \times 3.0\text{ m} = 18\text{ J}$. **Common mistake:** Using the mass ($4.0$) in the calculation, which is not required for work done ($6 \times 3 = 18$).

questions.question: Which of the following is equivalent to the work done on an object?
options: The energy transferred to the object
The force divided by the distance moved
The product of mass and acceleration
questions.explanation: The text states that work done is equal to the energy transferred ($Delta E$). **Common mistake:** Selecting 'rate of energy transfer', which defines power.

questions.question: A $200\text{ kg}$ load is lifted vertically through a distance of $30\text{ m}$. Calculate the work done against gravity. (Take $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$)
questions.explanation: $W = mgh = 200 \times 10 \times 30 = 60000\text{ J} = 60\text{ kJ}$. **Common mistake:** Failing to convert $60000\text{ J}$ to $60\text{ kJ}$ and choosing^6000 instead.

questions.question: A car of mass $1000\text{ kg}$ travels at a constant speed of $10\text{ m/s}$ along a horizontal road. If the driving force is $500\text{ N}$, how much work is done by the driving force in $20\text{ s}$?
questions.explanation: Step 1: find distance $s = vt = 10\text{ m/s} \times 20\text{ s} = 200\text{ m}$. Step 2: $W = Fs = 500\text{ N} \times 200\text{ m} = 100000\text{ J}$. **Common mistake:** Multiplying force and time ($500 \times 20 = 10000$) which is impulse, not work.

questions.question: A graph is plotted with Force ($y$-axis) against Distance moved ($x$-axis) for a constant force. What represents the work done?
options: The total length of the line
questions.explanation: Since $Work = Force \times Distance$, the product of the axes (area) represents the total work done. **Common mistake:** Thinking the gradient is work; the gradient would be zero for a constant force.
